      {
        "chunks": [
          {
            "content": "242: @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=False)\n243: def erase_nodes(gm: GraphModule, nodes: NodeList) -> None:\n244:     # erase original nodes in inversed topological order\n245:     for node in reversed(nodes):\n246:         gm.graph.erase_node(node)\n247: \n248: ",
            "endLine": 248,
            "startLine": 242
          },
          {
            "content": "136: \n137:     # validates partition doesn't introduce dependency circles in the graph\n138:     assert validate_partition(nodes), \"Invalid partition, found dependency cycles\"\n139: \n140:     # if no dict of partition nodes is provided, reconstruct it by nodes list to reduce lookup time\n141:     if partition_lookup_table is None:\n142:         partition_lookup_table = dict.fromkeys(nodes)\n143: \n144:     subgraph = Graph()\n145: \n146:     node_to_placeholder: dict[\n147:         Node, Node\n148:     ] = {}  # mapping of nodes from old graph to placeholder in new graph\n149:     node_map: dict[Node, Node] = {}  # mapping of nodes from old graph to new graph\n150: \n151:     # handles inputs through graph.node_copy's arg_transform functions\n152:     def remap_inputs(x: Node) -> Node:\n153:         if x.op == \"get_attr\":\n154:             # TODO: do we really need copy the get_attr node into the graph?\n155:             # do something here\n156: ",
            "endLine": 156,
            "startLine": 136
          },
          {
            "content": "158:         if x in partition_lookup_table:\n159:             # x is inside subgraph, return the copied node\n160:             # the node should have been copied already, as we are copying graph in the topological order\n161:             return node_map[x]\n162: \n163:         if x not in node_to_placeholder:\n164:             # x is not in subgraph, create a new placeholder for subgraph\n165:             placeholder_node = subgraph.placeholder(x.name, type_expr=x.type)\n166:             # copy all meta fields, even if some fields might be irrelevant for the placeholder node\n167:             placeholder_node.meta = copy.copy(x.meta)\n168:             node_to_placeholder[x] = placeholder_node\n169: \n170:         return node_to_placeholder[x]\n171: \n172:     # copy nodes in topological order\n173:     for node in nodes:\n174:         new_node = subgraph.node_copy(node, remap_inputs)\n175:         node_map[node] = new_node\n176: \n177: ",
            "endLine": 177,
            "startLine": 158
          }
        ],

            "content": "191:     tracer_extras = body.get(\"_tracer_extras\", {})\n192:     graph = KeepModules().trace(com, **tracer_extras)\n193: \n194:     # Recover node.meta[\"stack_trace\"] after re-tracing\n195:     node_meta_stack_trace = body.get(\"_graphmodule_graph_node_meta_stack_trace\", None)\n196:     if node_meta_stack_trace is not None:\n197:         del body[\"_graphmodule_graph_node_meta_stack_trace\"]\n198:         for node in graph.nodes:\n199:             if node_meta_stack_trace.get(node.name, None) is not None:\n200:                 node.meta[\"stack_trace\"] = node_meta_stack_trace[node.name]\n201: \n202:     # Manually set Tracer class on the reconstructed Graph, to avoid\n203:     # referencing the private local subclass KeepModules.\n204:     graph._tracer_cls = tracer_cls\n205:     from ._lazy_graph_module import _make_graph_module\n206: \n207:     gm = _make_graph_module(\n208:         com, graph, class_name=graphmodule_cls_name, graph_module_cls=graph_module_cls\n209:     )\n210: ",

            "content": "458: \n459:     mkldnn_graphs: dict[int, MklSubgraph] = defaultdict(lambda: MklSubgraph(fx_graph))\n460:     for node in fx_graph.nodes:\n461:         if hasattr(node, \"color\"):\n462:             mkldnn_graphs[uf.find(node.color)].nodes.append(node)\n463:         if hasattr(node, \"start_color\"):\n464:             mkldnn_graphs[uf.find(node.start_color)].start_nodes.append(node)\n465:         if hasattr(node, \"end_color\"):\n466:             mkldnn_graphs[uf.find(node.end_color)].end_nodes.append(node)\n467: \n468:     # Now that we have all the subgraphs, we need to decide which MKLDNN\n469:     # subgraphs we actually want to keep in MKLDNN.\n470:     for graph in mkldnn_graphs.values():\n471:         if not use_mkl_heuristic(graph):\n472:             for node in graph.start_nodes + graph.end_nodes:\n473:                 prv = node.args[0]\n474:                 node.replace_all_uses_with(prv)  # type: ignore[arg-type]\n475:                 fx_graph.erase_node(node)\n476:             reset_modules(graph.nodes, modules, old_modules)\n477: \n478: ",

      {
        "chunks": [
          {
            "content": "50: \n51: def _replace_with_hop(node: torch.fx.Node) -> None:\n52:     assert node.op == \"call_module\"\n53:     graph: torch.fx.Graph = node.graph\n54:     assert graph.owning_module is not None\n55:     gm: torch.fx.GraphModule = graph.owning_module\n56:     assert isinstance(node.target, str)\n57:     sub_gm = getattr(gm, node.target)\n58:     sub_graph = sub_gm.graph\n59:     set_grad_nodes = nodes_filter(sub_graph.nodes, _is_set_grad_enabled_node)\n60:     if len(set_grad_nodes) > 0:\n61:         assert len(set_grad_nodes) == 1\n62:         set_grad_node = set_grad_nodes[0]\n63:         _replace_with_hop_helper(node, set_grad_node, wrap_with_set_grad_enabled)\n64:         sub_graph.erase_node(set_grad_node)\n65: \n66: ",
            "endLine": 66,
            "startLine": 50
          },
          {
            "content": "66: \n67: def _remove_set_grad_and_inline(node: torch.fx.Node) -> None:\n68:     assert node.op == \"call_module\"\n69:     graph: torch.fx.Graph = node.graph\n70:     assert graph.owning_module is not None\n71:     gm: torch.fx.GraphModule = graph.owning_module\n72:     assert isinstance(node.target, str)\n73:     sub_gm = getattr(gm, node.target)\n74:     sub_graph = sub_gm.graph\n75:     nodes_map(\n76:         sub_graph.nodes,\n77:         lambda n: sub_graph.erase_node(n) if _is_set_grad_enabled_node(n) else n,\n78:     )\n79:     node_inline_(node)\n80: \n81: ",
            "endLine": 81,
            "startLine": 66
          },
          {
            "content": "29: def _is_set_grad_enabled_sub_mod(\n30:     node: torch.fx.Node, omit_if_same_with_ambient: bool = False\n31: ) -> Union[bool, torch.Tensor]:\n32:     if node.op == \"call_module\":\n33:         assert isinstance(node.target, str)\n34:         subgm = getattr(node.graph.owning_module, node.target)\n35:         first_non_ph = nodes_first(\n36:             subgm.graph.nodes, lambda node: node.op != \"placeholder\"\n37:         )\n38: ",
            "endLine": 38,
            "startLine": 29
          }
        ],

          },
          {
            "content": "39:     }\n40:     for gm in graph_module.modules():\n41:         for node in gm.graph.nodes:  # type: ignore[union-attr]\n42:             if node.op == \"call_function\" and node.target in aten_assertion_targets:\n43:                 gm.graph.erase_node(node)  # type: ignore[operator, union-attr]\n44:         gm.recompile()  # type: ignore[operator]\n45:     return graph_module\n46: ",
            "endLine": 46,

            "content": "21: \n22: def insert_type_promotion_nodes(\n23:     graph_module: torch.fx.GraphModule,\n24: ) -> None:\n25:     \"\"\"Inplace pass to insert explicit type promotion nodes, recursively through nested modules.\"\"\"\n26:     for module in graph_module.modules():\n27:         assert isinstance(module, torch.fx.GraphModule)\n28:         passes.InsertTypePromotion(module).run()\n29: ",

48. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
  "limit": 100,
  "offset": 990
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "            return [*self.table[(op, None)].keys()]\n\n        # op is call_method, get_attr, call_module\n        return [node for node in self.table[(op, None)].keys() if node.target == target]\n\n\n@compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\nclass Graph:\n    \"\"\"\n    ``Graph`` is the main data structure used in the FX Intermediate Representation.\n    It consists of a series of ``Node`` s, each representing callsites (or other\n    syntactic constructs). The list of ``Node`` s, taken together, constitute a\n    valid Python function.\n\n    For example, the following code\n\n    .. code-block:: python\n\n        import torch\n        import torch.fx\n\n\n        class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):\n            def __init__(self):\n                super().__init__()\n                self.param = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.rand(3, 4))\n                self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(4, 5)\n\n            def forward(self, x):\n                return torch.topk(\n                    torch.sum(self.linear(x + self.linear.weight).relu(), dim=-1), 3\n                )\n\n\n        m = MyModule()\n        gm = torch.fx.symbolic_trace(m)\n\n    Will produce the following Graph::\n\n        print(gm.graph)\n\n    .. code-block:: text\n\n        graph(x):\n            %linear_weight : [num_users=1] = self.linear.weight\n            %add_1 : [num_users=1] = call_function[target=operator.add](args = (%x, %linear_weight), kwargs = {})\n            %linear_1 : [num_users=1] = call_module[target=linear](args = (%add_1,), kwargs = {})\n            %relu_1 : [num_users=1] = call_method[target=relu](args = (%linear_1,), kwargs = {})\n            %sum_1 : [num_users=1] = call_function[target=torch.sum](args = (%relu_1,), kwargs = {dim: -1})\n            %topk_1 : [num_users=1] = call_function[target=torch.topk](args = (%sum_1, 3), kwargs = {})\n            return topk_1\n\n    For the semantics of operations represented in the ``Graph``, please see :class:`Node`.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def __init__(\n        self,\n        owning_module: Optional[\"GraphModule\"] = None,\n        tracer_cls: Optional[type[\"Tracer\"]] = None,\n        tracer_extras: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,\n    ):\n        \"\"\"\n        Construct an empty Graph.\n        \"\"\"\n        self._root: Node = Node(self, \"\", \"root\", \"\", (), {})\n        self._used_names: dict[str, int] = {}  # base name -> number\n        self._insert = self._root.prepend\n        self._len = 0\n        self._graph_namespace = _Namespace()\n        self._owning_module = owning_module\n        self._tracer_cls = tracer_cls\n        self._tracer_extras = tracer_extras\n        self._codegen = CodeGen()\n        self._co_fields: dict[str, Any] = {}\n        self._find_nodes_lookup_table = _FindNodesLookupTable()\n\n    @property\n    def owning_module(self):\n        return self._owning_module\n\n    @owning_module.setter\n    def owning_module(self, mod: Optional[\"GraphModule\"]):\n        self._owning_module = mod\n\n    @property\n    def nodes(self) -> _node_list:\n        \"\"\"\n        Get the list of Nodes that constitute this Graph.\n\n        Note that this ``Node`` list representation is a doubly-linked list. Mutations\n        during iteration (e.g. delete a Node, add a Node) are safe.\n\n        Returns:\n\n            A doubly-linked list of Nodes. Note that ``reversed`` can be called on\n            this list to switch iteration order.\n        \"\"\"\n        return _node_list(self)\n",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
    "numLines": 100,
    "startLine": 990,
    "totalLines": 2115
  },
  "type": "text"
}

\\n13: class TORCH_API LazyGraphExecutor {\\n14:  public:\\n15: \",\"endLine\":15,\"startLine\":12},{\"content\":\"22: \\n23:   // Register a lazy graph executor instance that can be retrieved using \",\"endLine\":494,\"startLine\":490},{\"content\":\"537:     // We have no Module, so we're just going to inline everything.\\n538:     // This should give us a totally flat graph.\\n539: lambdaLiftBlocksAndConvertToGraph(graph->block());\\n545:     createMethodCalls(graph);\\n546:     runCleanupPasses(self);\\n547:     // `graph` isn't referenced in `self` yet, so we need to stack is mutated, and we replay these mutations, we don't need\\n46: # any cleanup logic to be run once the graph break occurs, we simply replay\\n47: # these mutations to ensure at the graph break the torch function mode stack is correct\\n48: #  and reconstruct the torch function mode stack normally\\n49: \",\"endLine\":49,\"startLine\":45},{\"content\":\"40: \\n41: # These classes handle support for TorchFunctionModes across\\n42: # graph breaks\\n43: # Today the TorchFunctionMode enter (for the classes we support)\\n44: # simply pushes the mode onto the stack.             "content": "879:   // preserved when it escapes (meaning it is returned).\n880:   void handleSharedClassType(Module& module, std::shared_ptr<Graph>& graph) {\n881:     auto type = module.type();\n882: ",

          {
            "content": "802:   // 3) Remove non public unreferenced methods.\n803:   void cleanupFrozenModule() {\n804:     for (auto function : preservedMethods_) {\n805:       auto graph = toGraphFunction(*function).graph();\n806:       recordReferencedAttrs(graph);\n807:       handleSharedClassType(module_, graph);\n808:       removeExtraWaitCalls(graph->block());\n809:       toGraphFunction(*function).clear_optimized_graphs();\n810:     }\n811: ",
            "endLine": 811,
            "startLine": 802
          },
          {

50. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py",
  "limit": 50,
  "offset": 1550
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "        cg.restore_stack(stack_values, value_from_source=not tx.export)\n        self.side_effects.codegen_update_mutated(cg)\n\n    def cleanup_graph(self) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Remove \"creation_timestamp\" from node meta\n\n        Remove this pattern from the graph:\n            torch._C._set_grad_enabled(False)\n            torch._C._set_grad_enabled(True)\n        \"\"\"\n        assert self.should_exit\n        nodes = list(self.graph.nodes)\n        for node in nodes:\n            node.meta.pop(\"creation_timestamp\", None)\n\n        grad_enabled = torch.is_grad_enabled()\n        for node1, node2 in zip(nodes, nodes[1:]):\n            if (\n                node1.target is torch._C._set_grad_enabled\n                and tuple(node1.args) == (not grad_enabled,)\n                and not node1._erased\n            ):\n                grad_enabled = node1.args[0]\n                if (\n                    node2.target is torch._C._set_grad_enabled\n                    and tuple(node2.args) == (not grad_enabled,)\n                    and not node2._erased\n                ):\n                    grad_enabled = node2.args[0]\n                    self.graph.erase_node(node1)\n                    self.graph.erase_node(node2)\n\n    def get_graph_sizes_structured(self) -> dict[str, list[Union[int, str]]]:\n        ret: dict[str, list[Union[int, str]]] = {}\n        for node in self.graph.nodes:\n            example_value = node.meta.get(\"example_value\", None)\n            if isinstance(example_value, torch._subclasses.FakeTensor):\n                size = example_value.size()\n                ret[node.name] = [s if isinstance(s, int) else repr(s) for s in size]\n        return ret\n\n    def get_graph_sizes(self, name: str) -> str:\n        graph_sizes_str = \"TRACED GRAPH TENSOR SIZES\\n\"\n        graph_sizes_str += f\"===== {name} =====\\n\"\n        for node in self.graph.nodes:\n            example_value = node.meta.get(\"example_value\", None)\n            if isinstance(example_value, torch._subclasses.FakeTensor):\n                size = example_value.size()\n                graph_sizes_str += f\"{node.name}: {tuple(size)}\\n\"",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py",
    "numLines": 50,
    "startLine": 1550,
    "totalLines": 3273
  },
  "type": "text"
}

{
  "file": {
    "content": "                )\n            )\n            stack.enter_context(CompileTimeInstructionCounter.record())\n            return _compile_inner(code, one_graph, hooks, transform)\n\n        return (\n            ConvertFrameReturn()\n        )  # dead, but see https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7577\n\n    @maybe_cprofile\n    def _compile_inner(\n        code: CodeType,\n        one_graph: bool,\n        hooks: Hooks,\n        transform: Callable[[list[Instruction], dict[str, Any]], Any],\n    ) -> ConvertFrameReturn:\n        nonlocal dynamo_time_before_restart\n        last_attempt_start_time = start_time = time.time()\n\n        def log_bytecode(\n            prefix: str, name: str, filename: str, line_no: int, code: CodeType\n        ) -> None:\n            if bytecode_log.isEnabledFor(logging.DEBUG):\n                bytecode_log.debug(\n                    format_bytecode(prefix, name, filename, line_no, code)\n                )\n\n        log_bytecode(\n            \"ORIGINAL BYTECODE\",\n            code.co_name,\n            code.co_filename,\n            code.co_firstlineno,\n            code,\n        )\n\n        out_code = None\n        for attempt in itertools.count():\n            CompileContext.get().attempt = attempt\n            try:\n                with dynamo_timed(\n                    f\"compile_attempt_{attempt}\", log_pt2_compile_event=True\n                ):\n                    out_code = transform_code_object(code, transform)\n                break\n            except exc.RestartAnalysis as e:\n                if not isinstance(e, exc.TensorifyScalarRestartAnalysis):\n                    TensorifyState.clear()\n                log.info(\n                    \"Restarting analysis due to %s\",\n                    LazyString(format_traceback_short, e.__traceback__),\n                )\n                # If restart reason is None just log the type of the exception\n                restart_reasons.add(e.restart_reason or str(type(e)))\n                # We now have a new \"last attempt\", reset the clock\n                last_attempt_start_time = time.time()\n                if attempt > 100:\n                    unimplemented_v2(\n                        gb_type=\"Excessive RestartAnalysis() calls\",\n                        context=\"\",\n                        explanation=\"Dynamo attempted to trace the same frame 100+ times. \"\n                        \"Giving up on compiling as the compile time tradeoff is likely not \"\n                        \"worth the performance gain.\",\n                        hints=[],\n                    )\n            except exc.SkipFrame as e:\n                if not isinstance(e, exc.TensorifyScalarRestartAnalysis):\n                    TensorifyState.clear()\n                log.debug(\n                    \"Skipping frame %s %s \\\n                    %s %s\",\n                    e,\n                    code.co_name,\n                    code.co_filename,\n                    code.co_firstlineno,\n                )\n                if one_graph or _is_error_on_graph_break(tracer):\n                    log.debug(\n                        \"No graph captured with one_graph=True or error_on_graph_break=True\"\n                    )\n                return ConvertFrameReturn()\n\n        assert distributed_state is None or distributed_state.all_states is not None, (  # type: ignore[has-type]\n            \"compiler collective wasn't run before compilation completed\"\n        )\n\n        assert out_code is not None\n        log_bytecode(\n            \"MODIFIED BYTECODE\",\n            code.co_name,\n            code.co_filename,\n            code.co_firstlineno,\n            out_code,\n        )\n\n        for idx, hook in enumerate(_bytecode_hooks.values()):\n            with dynamo_timed(f\"bytecode_hooks_{idx}\", log_pt2_compile_event=True):\n                hook_output = hook(code, out_code)\n                if hook_output is not None:\n                    out_code = hook_output\n",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py",
    "numLines": 100,
    "startLine": 860,
    "totalLines": 1642
  },
  "type": "text"
}

54. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/node.py",
  "limit": 50,
  "offset": 425
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "        Args:\n            x (Node): The node to put after this node. Must be a member of the same graph.\n        \"\"\"\n        self._next.prepend(x)\n\n    def _remove_from_list(self) -> None:\n        p, n = self._prev, self._next\n        p._next, n._prev = n, p\n\n    @property\n    def args(self) -> tuple[Argument, ...]:\n        \"\"\"\n        The tuple of arguments to this ``Node``. The interpretation of arguments\n        depends on the node's opcode. See the :class:`Node` docstring for more\n        information.\n\n        Assignment to this property is allowed. All accounting of uses and users\n        is updated automatically on assignment.\n        \"\"\"\n        return self._args\n\n    @args.setter\n    def args(self, a: tuple[Argument, ...]) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Set the tuple of arguments to this Node. The interpretation of arguments\n        depends on the node's opcode. See the ``fx.Graph`` docstring for more\n        information.\n        \"\"\"\n        # DO NOT CALL `_update_args_kwargs` directly. The correct way to\n        # set `args` is via direct assignment, i.e. `node.args = new_args`\n        self._update_args_kwargs(a, self._kwargs)\n\n    @property\n    def kwargs(self) -> dict[str, Argument]:\n        \"\"\"\n        The dict of keyword arguments to this ``Node``. The interpretation of arguments\n        depends on the node's opcode. See the :class:`Node` docstring for more\n        information.\n\n        Assignment to this property is allowed. All accounting of uses and users\n        is updated automatically on assignment.\n        \"\"\"\n        return self._kwargs\n\n    @kwargs.setter\n    def kwargs(self, k: dict[str, Argument]) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Set the dict of kwargs to this Node. The interpretation of arguments\n        depends on the node's opcode. See the ``fx.Graph`` docstring for more\n        information.",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/node.py",
    "numLines": 50,
    "startLine": 425,
    "totalLines": 941
  },
  "type": "text"
}
55. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_deepsearch_read @ -

{
  "identifier": "https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/deepsearch/dc230087-137f-40c7-9cae-6460a5339517"
}
Output
{
  "content": "{\"markdown\":\"---\\ntitle: How does the dynamo tracer output cleanup work in PyTorch? I need to understand the reference cycles that occur when tracer fails: 1) Graph nodes forming a doubly linked list pointing back to the graph, 2) FakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode cycle. How can these cycles be manually cleaned up to allow immediate garbage collection?\\nshare_url: https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/deepsearch/dc230087-137f-40c7-9cae-6460a5339517\\ncreated: 2026-02-05T22:29:04Z\\nupdated: 2026-02-05T22:31:25Z\\n---\\n\\n# How does the dynamo tracer output cleanup work in PyTorch? I need to understand the reference cycles that occur when tracer fails: 1) Graph nodes forming a doubly linked list pointing back to the graph, 2) FakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode cycle. How can these cycles be manually cleaned up to allow immediate garbage collection?\\n\\nExcellent! Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive answer to the user.\\n\\n## PyTorch Dynamo Tracer Output Cleanup and Reference Cycles\\n\\nThe dynamo tracer cleanup mechanism in PyTorch addresses two critical reference cycles that prevent immediate garbage collection when the tracer fails. Here's how it works:\\n\\n### The Two Reference Cycles\\n\\n**1. Graph Nodes Doubly Linked List Cycle**\\n\\nThe FX `Node` objects form an intrusive doubly linked list where each node points to its previous and next nodes (`_prev` and `_next`), and all nodes hold a reference back to their parent `Graph`. This creates a cycle:\\n\\n```\\nNode \u2194 _prev/_next (other Nodes) \u2192 Graph \u2192 Node\\n```\\n\\nThe C++ implementation in [node.cpp](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L154-L172) shows the structure:\\n\\n```cpp\\nstruct NodeBase {\\n  PyObject_HEAD\\n  bool _erased;\\n  NodeBase* _prev;      // Points to previous node\\n  NodeBase* _next;      // Points to next node\\n  PyObject* graph;      // Points back to parent graph\\n  // ... other fields\\n};\\n```\\n\\n**2. FakeTensorMode Cycle**\\n\\nThe second cycle occurs through the fake tensor infrastructure:\\n\\n```\\nFakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 tracked_fakes (list) \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode\\n```\\n\\nThis happens because [`OutputGraph` shares the `tracked_fakes` list](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L606-L616) with `ShapeEnv`:\\n\\n```python\\nself.tracked_fakes: list[TrackedFake] = []\\n\\nshape_env = ShapeEnv(\\n    # Reference Cycle!\\n    # Share a reference to the list of TrackedFake.\\n    tracked_fakes=self.tracked_fakes,\\n    # ...\\n)\\n```\\n\\n### Manual Cleanup Implementation\\n\\nThe cleanup is performed in [`_cleanup_output_graph()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2989-L2999) and called when the tracer fails (in [convert_frame.py](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py?L1355-L1360)):\\n\\n```python\\ndef _cleanup_output_graph(self) -> None:\\n    output_graph = self.output_graph_for_cleanup\\n    if output_graph:\\n        # 1. Break the doubly linked list cycles\\n        for tracer in output_graph.tracers:\\n            tracer.graph._clear_nodes()\\n        \\n        # 2. Break the FakeTensorMode cycle\\n        if (\\n            output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode\\n            and output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env\\n        ):\\n            output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env.tracked_fakes = None\\n```\\n\\n**Breaking the Node Cycles:**\\n\\n[`_clear_nodes()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L2284-L2287) iterates in reverse and erases each node:\\n\\n```python\\ndef _clear_nodes(self) -> None:\\n    for node in reversed(self.nodes):\\n        node.meta.clear()\\n        self.erase_node(node)\\n```\\n\\n[`erase_node()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L1487-L1525) does several cleanup operations:\\n\\n1. Calls [`_remove_from_list()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L196-L204) to unlink from the doubly linked list\\n2. Nulls out the node's arguments to break references to other nodes:\\n\\n```python\\n# Null out this Node's argument nodes so that the Nodes referred to\\n# can update their ``users`` accordingly\\nto_erase._update_args_kwargs(\\n    map_arg(to_erase._args, lambda n: None),\\n    map_arg(to_erase._kwargs, lambda n: None),\\n)\\n```\\n\\nThe C++ [`remove_from_list()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L196-L204) updates the doubly linked list pointers:\\n\\n```cpp\\ninline void remove_from_list() {\\n    if (this->_prev == this && this->_next == this) {\\n        return;\\n    }\\n    NodeBase* p = this->_prev;\\n    NodeBase* n = this->_next;\\n    p->set_next(n);  // Previous points to next\\n    n->set_prev(p);  // Next points to previous\\n}\\n```\\n\\n**Breaking the FakeTensor Cycle:**\\n\\nSimply set `tracked_fakes` to `None` to break the `ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor` chain:\\n\\n```python\\noutput_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env.tracked_fakes = None\\n```\\n\\n### Why This Matters\\n\\nWithout manual cleanup, these cycles would only be freed by Python's cyclic garbage collector, which:\\n- Runs non-deterministically\\n- Can delay memory reclamation significantly\\n- May cause OOM errors in tight loops (like the failing Python 3.14 tests that were [fixed by this change](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/7dfaefdafc79348c7d8cfe7a520b3f9ef3a04549))\\n\\nThe manual cleanup allows **immediate garbage collection** by breaking the cycles explicitly, ensuring that tensor memory and other resources are freed as soon as the tracer output goes out of scope.\\n\\n### Key Commit\\n\\nThis cleanup mechanism was added in commit [7dfaefd](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/7dfaefdafc79348c7d8cfe7a520b3f9ef3a04549) to fix Python 3.14 failures caused by accumulated memory from failed tracer outputs.\\n\\n## Sources\\n\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py@d232676?L2980-3020](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2980-3020)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py@d232676?L2885-2920](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2885-2920)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/graph.py@d232676?L2280-2295](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L2280-2295)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/graph.py@d232676?L1480-1530](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L1480-1530)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp@d232676?L350-380](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L350-380)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py@d232676?L3775-3800](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py?L3775-3800)\\n- [repo:^github.com/pytorch/pytorch$ file:output_graph.py tracked_fakes](/search?q=patterntype:keyword%20repo:%5Egithub.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch$%20file:output_graph.py%20tracked_fakes)\\n- [repo:^github.com/pytorch/pytorch$ file:symbolic_shapes.py tracked_fakes](/search?q=patterntype:keyword%20repo:%5Egithub.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch$%20file:symbolic_shapes.py%20tracked_fakes)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/node.py@d232676?L100-150](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/node.py?L100-150)\\n- file:symbolic_shapes.py class TrackedFake](/search?q=patterntype:keyword%20repo:%5Egithub.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch$%20file:symbolic_shapes.py%20class%20TrackedFake)\\n- graph\\\" OR message:\\\"reference cycle\\\") repo:github.com/pytorch/pytorch count:50](/search?q=type:commit%20%28message:cleanup%20OR%20message:%22output%20graph%22%20OR%20message:%22reference%20cycle%22%29%20repo:github.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch%20count:50)\\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch (diff 7dfaefd~1...7dfaefd)](/github.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch/-/compare/7dfaefd~1..7dfaefd)\\n\\n\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "markdown": "---\ntitle: How does the dynamo tracer output cleanup work in PyTorch? I need to understand the reference cycles that occur when tracer fails: 1) Graph nodes forming a doubly linked list pointing back to the graph, 2) FakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode cycle. How can these cycles be manually cleaned up to allow immediate garbage collection?\nshare_url: https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/deepsearch/dc230087-137f-40c7-9cae-6460a5339517\ncreated: 2026-02-05T22:29:04Z\nupdated: 2026-02-05T22:31:25Z\n---\n\n# How does the dynamo tracer output cleanup work in PyTorch? I need to understand the reference cycles that occur when tracer fails: 1) Graph nodes forming a doubly linked list pointing back to the graph, 2) FakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode cycle. How can these cycles be manually cleaned up to allow immediate garbage collection?\n\nExcellent! Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive answer to the user.\n\n## PyTorch Dynamo Tracer Output Cleanup and Reference Cycles\n\nThe dynamo tracer cleanup mechanism in PyTorch addresses two critical reference cycles that prevent immediate garbage collection when the tracer fails. Here's how it works:\n\n### The Two Reference Cycles\n\n**1. Graph Nodes Doubly Linked List Cycle**\n\nThe FX `Node` objects form an intrusive doubly linked list where each node points to its previous and next nodes (`_prev` and `_next`), and all nodes hold a reference back to their parent `Graph`. This creates a cycle:\n\n```\nNode \u2194 _prev/_next (other Nodes) \u2192 Graph \u2192 Node\n```\n\nThe C++ implementation in [node.cpp](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L154-L172) shows the structure:\n\n```cpp\nstruct NodeBase {\n  PyObject_HEAD\n  bool _erased;\n  NodeBase* _prev;      // Points to previous node\n  NodeBase* _next;      // Points to next node\n  PyObject* graph;      // Points back to parent graph\n  // ... other fields\n};\n```\n\n**2. FakeTensorMode Cycle**\n\nThe second cycle occurs through the fake tensor infrastructure:\n\n```\nFakeTensorMode \u2192 ShapeEnv \u2192 tracked_fakes (list) \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor \u2192 FakeTensorMode\n```\n\nThis happens because [`OutputGraph` shares the `tracked_fakes` list](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L606-L616) with `ShapeEnv`:\n\n```python\nself.tracked_fakes: list[TrackedFake] = []\n\nshape_env = ShapeEnv(\n    # Reference Cycle!\n    # Share a reference to the list of TrackedFake.\n    tracked_fakes=self.tracked_fakes,\n    # ...\n)\n```\n\n### Manual Cleanup Implementation\n\nThe cleanup is performed in [`_cleanup_output_graph()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2989-L2999) and called when the tracer fails (in [convert_frame.py](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py?L1355-L1360)):\n\n```python\ndef _cleanup_output_graph(self) -> None:\n    output_graph = self.output_graph_for_cleanup\n    if output_graph:\n        # 1. Break the doubly linked list cycles\n        for tracer in output_graph.tracers:\n            tracer.graph._clear_nodes()\n        \n        # 2. Break the FakeTensorMode cycle\n        if (\n            output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode\n            and output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env\n        ):\n            output_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env.tracked_fakes = None\n```\n\n**Breaking the Node Cycles:**\n\n[`_clear_nodes()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L2284-L2287) iterates in reverse and erases each node:\n\n```python\ndef _clear_nodes(self) -> None:\n    for node in reversed(self.nodes):\n        node.meta.clear()\n        self.erase_node(node)\n```\n\n[`erase_node()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L1487-L1525) does several cleanup operations:\n\n1. Calls [`_remove_from_list()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L196-L204) to unlink from the doubly linked list\n2. Nulls out the node's arguments to break references to other nodes:\n\n```python\n# Null out this Node's argument nodes so that the Nodes referred to\n# can update their ``users`` accordingly\nto_erase._update_args_kwargs(\n    map_arg(to_erase._args, lambda n: None),\n    map_arg(to_erase._kwargs, lambda n: None),\n)\n```\n\nThe C++ [`remove_from_list()`](https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/pytorch/pytorch/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L196-L204) updates the doubly linked list pointers:\n\n```cpp\ninline void remove_from_list() {\n    if (this->_prev == this && this->_next == this) {\n        return;\n    }\n    NodeBase* p = this->_prev;\n    NodeBase* n = this->_next;\n    p->set_next(n);  // Previous points to next\n    n->set_prev(p);  // Next points to previous\n}\n```\n\n**Breaking the FakeTensor Cycle:**\n\nSimply set `tracked_fakes` to `None` to break the `ShapeEnv \u2192 TrackedFake \u2192 FakeTensor` chain:\n\n```python\noutput_graph.tracing_context.fake_mode.shape_env.tracked_fakes = None\n```\n\n### Why This Matters\n\nWithout manual cleanup, these cycles would only be freed by Python's cyclic garbage collector, which:\n- Runs non-deterministically\n- Can delay memory reclamation significantly\n- May cause OOM errors in tight loops (like the failing Python 3.14 tests that were [fixed by this change](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/7dfaefdafc79348c7d8cfe7a520b3f9ef3a04549))\n\nThe manual cleanup allows **immediate garbage collection** by breaking the cycles explicitly, ensuring that tensor memory and other resources are freed as soon as the tracer output goes out of scope.\n\n### Key Commit\n\nThis cleanup mechanism was added in commit [7dfaefd](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/7dfaefdafc79348c7d8cfe7a520b3f9ef3a04549) to fix Python 3.14 failures caused by accumulated memory from failed tracer outputs.\n\n## Sources\n\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py@d232676?L2980-3020](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2980-3020)\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py@d232676?L2885-2920](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py?L2885-2920)\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/graph.py@d232676?L2280-2295](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L2280-2295)\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/graph.py@d232676?L1480-1530](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/graph.py?L1480-1530)\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp@d232676?L350-380](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/csrc/fx/node.cpp?L350-380)\n- [github.com/pytorch/pytorch/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py@d232676?L3775-3800](/github.com/pytorch/pytorch@d232676bfd7f7bd6436fddfeccf6aa4fc5a70259/-/blob/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py?L3775-3800)\n- [repo:^github.com/pytorch/pytorch$ file:output_graph.py tracked_fakes](/search?q=patterntype:keyword%20repo:%5Egithub.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch$%20file:output_graph.py%20tracked_fakes)\n- [repo:^github.com/pytorch/pytorch$ file:symbolic_shapes.py tracked_fakes](/search?q=patterntype:keyword%20repo:%5Egithub.com%2Fpytorch%2Fpytorch$%20file:symbolic_shapes.py%20tracked_fakes)\n- {
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",

Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "            )\n        return self.create_node(\n            \"call_module\", module_name, args, kwargs, type_expr=type_expr\n        )\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def call_method(\n        self,\n        method_name: str,\n        args: Optional[tuple[\"Argument\", ...]] = None,\n        kwargs: Optional[dict[str, \"Argument\"]] = None,\n        type_expr: Optional[Any] = None,\n    ) -> Node:\n        \"\"\"\n        Insert a ``call_method`` ``Node`` into the ``Graph``. A ``call_method`` node\n        represents a call to a given method on the 0th element of ``args``.\n\n        Args:\n\n            method_name (str): The name of the method to apply to the self argument.\n                For example, if args[0] is a ``Node`` representing a ``Tensor``,\n                then to call ``relu()`` on that ``Tensor``, pass ``relu`` to ``method_name``.\n\n            args (Optional[Tuple[Argument, ...]]): The positional arguments to be passed\n                to the called method. Note that this *should* include a ``self`` argument.\n\n            kwargs (Optional[Dict[str, Argument]]): The keyword arguments to be passed\n                to the called method\n\n            type_expr (Optional[Any]): an optional type annotation representing the\n                Python type the output of this node will have.\n\n        Returns:\n\n            The newly created and inserted ``call_method`` node.\n\n        .. note::\n            The same insertion point and type expression rules apply for this method\n            as :meth:`Graph.create_node`.\n        \"\"\"\n        return self.create_node(\n            \"call_method\", method_name, args, kwargs, type_expr=type_expr\n        )\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def call_function(\n        self,\n        the_function: Callable[..., Any],\n        args: Optional[tuple[\"Argument\", ...]] = None,\n        kwargs: Optional[dict[str, \"Argument\"]] = None,\n        type_expr: Optional[Any] = None,\n        name: Optional[str] = None,\n    ) -> Node:\n        \"\"\"\n        Insert a ``call_function`` ``Node`` into the ``Graph``. A ``call_function`` node\n        represents a call to a Python callable, specified by ``the_function``.\n\n        Args:\n\n            the_function (Callable[..., Any]): The function to be called. Can be any PyTorch\n                operator, Python function, or member of the ``builtins`` or ``operator``\n                namespaces.\n\n            args (Optional[Tuple[Argument, ...]]): The positional arguments to be passed\n                to the called function.\n\n            kwargs (Optional[Dict[str, Argument]]): The keyword arguments to be passed\n                to the called function\n\n            type_expr (Optional[Any]): an optional type annotation representing the\n                Python type the output of this node will have.\n\n            name (Optional[str]): The name of the node. If not specified, set to None\n\n        Returns:\n\n            The newly created and inserted ``call_function`` node.\n\n        .. note::\n            The same insertion point and type expression rules apply for this method",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 1480,
    "totalLines": 2115
  },
  "type": "text"
}
58. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
  "limit": 80,
  "offset": 1600
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def output(self, result: \"Argument\", type_expr: Optional[Any] = None):\n        \"\"\"\n        Insert an ``output`` ``Node`` into the ``Graph``. An ``output`` node represents\n        a ``return`` statement in Python code. ``result`` is the value that should\n        be returned.\n\n        Args:\n\n            result (Argument): The value to be returned.\n\n            type_expr (Optional[Any]): an optional type annotation representing the\n                Python type the output of this node will have.\n\n        .. note::\n\n            The same insertion point and type expression rules apply for this method\n            as ``Graph.create_node``.\n        \"\"\"\n        return self.create_node(\n            op=\"output\", target=\"output\", args=(result,), type_expr=type_expr\n        )\n\n    def _target_to_str(self, target: Optional[Target]) -> str:\n        if callable(target):\n            op = target.__name__\n        else:\n            assert isinstance(target, str)\n            op = target\n            if _is_magic(op):\n                op = op[2:-2]\n        op = _snake_case(op)\n        return op\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def python_code(\n        self,\n        root_module: str,\n        *,\n        verbose: bool = False,\n        include_stride: bool = False,\n        include_device: bool = False,\n        colored: bool = False,\n        expanded_def: bool = False,\n    ) -> PythonCode:\n        \"\"\"\n        Turn this ``Graph`` into valid Python code.\n\n        Args:\n\n            root_module (str): The name of the root module on which to look-up\n                qualified name targets. This is usually 'self'.\n\n        Returns:\n\n            A PythonCode object, consisting of two fields:\n                src: the Python source code representing the object\n                globals: a dictionary of global names in `src` -> the objects that they reference.\n        \"\"\"\n        # NOTE: [Graph Namespaces]\n        #\n        # There are two types of symbols in generated Python source code:\n        # locals and globals.\n        #   Locals are locally defined by the output of a node in the Graph.\n        #   Globals are references to external objects, like functions or types.\n        #\n        # When generating Python code, we need to make sure to name things\n        # appropriately. In particular:\n        # - All names should be unique, to avoid weird shadowing bugs.\n        # - These names need to be consistent, e.g. a object should always be\n        #   referenced by the same name.\n        #\n        # To do this, we create a new namespace just for this source. All names\n        # that get printed must come from this namespace.\n        #\n        # Why can't we reuse node.name? Because it was generated within the\n        # namespace `self._graph_namespace`. In order to provide uniqueness\n        # over both locals (node.name) *and* globals, we create a completely\n        # new namespace to put all identifiers in.",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 1600,
    "totalLines": 2115
  },
  "type": "text"
}
59. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
  "limit": 80,
  "offset": 1320
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "\n        Args:\n\n            n (Optional[Node]): The node before which to insert. If None this will insert after\n                the beginning of the entire graph.\n\n        Returns:\n            A resource manager that will restore the insert point on ``__exit__``.\n        \"\"\"\n        if n is None:\n            return self.inserting_before(self._root)\n        assert n.graph == self, \"Node to insert after is not in graph.\"\n        return _InsertPoint(self, n.append)\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def placeholder(\n        self,\n        name: str,\n        type_expr: Optional[Any] = None,\n        default_value: Any = inspect.Signature.empty,\n    ) -> Node:\n        \"\"\"\n        Insert a ``placeholder`` node into the Graph. A ``placeholder`` represents\n        a function input.\n\n        Args:\n\n            name (str): A name for the input value. This corresponds to the name\n                of the positional argument to the function this ``Graph`` represents.\n\n            type_expr (Optional[Any]): an optional type annotation representing the\n                Python type the output of this node will have. This is needed in some\n                cases for proper code generation (e.g. when the function is used\n                subsequently in TorchScript compilation).\n\n            default_value (Any): The default value this function argument should take\n                on. NOTE: to allow for `None` as a default value, `inspect.Signature.empty`\n                should be passed as this argument to specify that the parameter does _not_\n                have a default value.\n\n        .. note::\n            The same insertion point and type expression rules apply for this method\n            as ``Graph.create_node``.\n        \"\"\"\n        args = () if default_value is inspect.Signature.empty else (default_value,)\n        return self.create_node(\"placeholder\", name, args=args, type_expr=type_expr)\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def get_attr(self, qualified_name: str, type_expr: Optional[Any] = None) -> Node:\n        \"\"\"\n        Insert a ``get_attr`` node into the Graph. A ``get_attr`` ``Node`` represents the\n        fetch of an attribute from the ``Module`` hierarchy.\n\n        Args:\n\n            qualified_name (str): the fully-qualified name of the attribute to be retrieved.\n                For example, if the traced Module has a submodule named ``foo``, which has a\n                submodule named ``bar``, which has an attribute named ``baz``, the qualified\n                name ``foo.bar.baz`` should be passed as ``qualified_name``.\n\n            type_expr (Optional[Any]): an optional type annotation representing the\n                Python type the output of this node will have.\n\n\n        Returns:\n\n            The newly-created and inserted ``get_attr`` node.\n\n        .. note::\n            The same insertion point and type expression rules apply for this method\n            as ``Graph.create_node``.\n        \"\"\"\n\n        def _get_attr_reference_exists(\n            mod: torch.nn.Module, qualified_name: str\n        ) -> bool:\n            module_path, _, name = qualified_name.rpartition(\".\")\n\n            try:\n                submod: torch.nn.Module = mod.get_submodule(module_path)",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 1320,
    "totalLines": 2115
  },
  "type": "text"
}

61. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
  "limit": 80,
  "offset": 1240
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "        return self._codegen.process_inputs(*args)\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=False)\n    def process_outputs(self, out):\n        return self._codegen.process_outputs(out)\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def erase_node(self, to_erase: Node) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Erases a ``Node`` from the ``Graph``. Throws an exception if\n        there are still users of that node in the ``Graph``.\n\n        Args:\n\n            to_erase (Node): The ``Node`` to erase from the ``Graph``.\n        \"\"\"\n        if len(to_erase.users) > 0:\n            raise RuntimeError(\n                f\"Tried to erase Node {to_erase} but it still had {len(to_erase.users)} \"\n                f\"users in the graph: {to_erase.users}!\"\n            )\n        if to_erase.graph != self:\n            raise RuntimeError(f\"Attempting to remove {to_erase} from wrong graph!\")\n        if to_erase._erased:\n            warnings.warn(f\"erase_node({to_erase}) on an already erased node\")\n            return\n\n        if (\n            self.owning_module is not None\n            and getattr(self.owning_module, \"_erase_node_hooks\", None) is not None\n        ):\n            for f in self.owning_module._erase_node_hooks:\n                f(to_erase)\n\n        self._find_nodes_lookup_table.remove(to_erase)\n        to_erase._remove_from_list()\n        to_erase._erased = True  # iterators may retain handles to erased nodes\n        self._len -= 1\n\n        # Null out this Node's argument nodes so that the Nodes referred to\n        # can update their ``users`` accordingly\n        to_erase._update_args_kwargs(\n            map_arg(to_erase._args, lambda n: None),\n            map_arg(to_erase._kwargs, lambda n: None),\n        )\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def inserting_before(self, n: Optional[Node] = None):\n        \"\"\"Set the point at which create_node and companion methods will insert into the graph.\n        When used within a 'with' statement, this will temporary set the insert point and\n        then restore it when the with statement exits::\n\n            with g.inserting_before(n):\n                ...  # inserting before node n\n            ...  # insert point restored to what it was previously\n            g.inserting_before(n)  #  set the insert point permanently\n\n        Args:\n\n            n (Optional[Node]): The node before which to insert. If None this will insert before\n                the beginning of the entire graph.\n\n        Returns:\n            A resource manager that will restore the insert point on ``__exit__``.\n        \"\"\"\n        if n is None:\n            return self.inserting_after(self._root)\n        assert n.graph == self, \"Node to insert before is not in graph.\"\n        return _InsertPoint(self, n.prepend)\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def inserting_after(self, n: Optional[Node] = None):\n        \"\"\"Set the point at which create_node and companion methods will insert into the graph.\n        When used within a 'with' statement, this will temporary set the insert point and\n        then restore it when the with statement exits::\n\n            with g.inserting_after(n):\n                ...  # inserting after node n\n            ...  # insert point restored to what it was previously\n            g.inserting_after(n)  #  set the insert point permanently",
    "filePath": "/workspace/torch/fx/graph.py",
    "numLines": 80,
    "startLine": 1240,
    "totalLines": 2115
  },
  "type": "text"
}

    "content": "                lambda current_trans: (\n                    lambda body: insert_pdb(\n                        current_trans(body) if current_trans else body\n                    )\n                )\n            )\n\n            gm.recompile()\n            gm(*inputs)  # drops into pdb\n\n\n        This function can also be used as a context manager, with the benefit to\n        automatically restores the previously registered code transformer:\n\n        .. code-block:: python\n\n            # ... continue from previous example\n\n            with gm.graph.on_generate_code(lambda _: insert_pdb):\n                # do more stuff with `gm`...\n                gm.recompile()\n                gm(*inputs)  # drops into pdb\n\n            # now previous code transformer is restored (but `gm`'s code with pdb\n            # remains - that means you can run `gm` with pdb here too, until you\n            # run next `recompile()`).\n        \"\"\"\n        on_gen_code_old = self._codegen._body_transformer\n        self._codegen._body_transformer = make_transformer(on_gen_code_old)\n\n        @contextlib.contextmanager\n        def on_generate_code_context_manager():\n            try:\n                yield\n            finally:\n                self._codegen._body_transformer = on_gen_code_old\n\n        return on_generate_code_context_manager()\n\n\n@contextmanager\ndef _override_sym_repr(\n    override: Callable[[\"torch.types.PySymType\"], str],\n) -> Iterator[None]:\n    tmp = CodeGen._sym_repr\n    try:\n        CodeGen._sym_repr = override\n        yield\n    finally:\n        CodeGen._sym_repr = tmp\n\n\ndef _identity(x):\n    return x\n\n\ndef _make_color_fn(code):\n    def f(s):\n        reset = \"\\033[0m\"\n        return f\"{code}{s}{reset}\"",

  "oldString": "        # Null out this Node's argument nodes so that the Nodes referred to\n        # can update their ``users`` accordingly\n        to_erase._update_args_kwargs(\n            map_arg(to_erase._args, lambda n: None),\n            map_arg(to_erase._kwargs, lambda n: None),\n        )\n\n    @compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n    def inserting_before(self, n: Optional[Node] = None):",
  "originalFile": "# mypy: allow-untyped-defs\nimport builtins\nimport contextlib\nimport copy\nimport enum\nimport functools\nimport inspect\nimport keyword\nimport math\nimport os\nimport re\nimport typing\nimport warnings\nfrom collections import defaultdict\nfrom collections.abc import Iterable, Iterator\nfrom contextlib import contextmanager\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass\nfrom typing import Any, Callable, Literal, NamedTuple, Optional, TYPE_CHECKING\n\nimport torch\nimport torch.utils._pytree as pytree\nfrom torch._C import _fx_map_arg as map_arg, _NodeIter\nfrom torch.utils._dtype_abbrs import dtype_abbrs\n\nfrom . import _pytree as fx_pytree\nfrom ._compatibility import compatibility\nfrom .immutable_collections import immutable_dict\nfrom .node import _get_qualified_name, _type_repr, Argument, Node, Target\n\n\n__all__ = [\"PythonCode\", \"CodeGen\", \"Graph\"]\n\nif TYPE_CHECKING:\n    from ._symbolic_trace import Tracer  # noqa: F401\n    from .graph_module import GraphModule  # noqa: F401\n\n\n# Mapping of builtins to their `typing` equivalent.\n# (PEP585: See D68459095 test plan)\n_origin_type_map = {\n    list: typing.List,  # noqa: UP006\n    dict: typing.Dict,  # noqa: UP006\n    set: typing.Set,  # noqa: UP006\n    frozenset: typing.FrozenSet,  # noqa: UP006\n    tuple: typing.Tuple,  # noqa: UP006\n}\n\n_legal_ops = dict.fromkeys(\n    [\"call_function\", \"call_method\", \"get_attr\", \"call_module\", \"placeholder\", \"output\"]\n)\n\n\n# Signature for functions thattransforms the body (`list[str]`) of the\n# generated code\nTransformCodeFunc = Callable[[list[str]], list[str]]\n\n\nclass _CustomBuiltin(NamedTuple):\n    \"\"\"Additional objs that we add to every graph's globals.\n\n    The repr() for some standard library objects is not valid Python code without\n    an import. For common objects of this sort, we bundle them in the globals of\n    every FX graph.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    # How to import this object from the standard library.\n    import_str: str\n    # The actual object, produced from that import string.\n    obj: Any\n\n\n# Combined dict of disallowed variable names so we can check with one lookup\n_illegal_names = {k: object() for k in keyword.kwlist}\n_illegal_names.update(builtins.__dict__)  # can't shadow a builtin name\n\n_custom_builtins: dict[str, _CustomBuiltin] = {}\n\n\ndef _register_custom_builtin(name: str, import_str: str, obj: Any):\n    _custom_builtins[name] = _CustomBuiltin(import_str, obj)\n    _illegal_names[name] = obj\n\n\n_register_custom_builtin(\"inf\", \"from math import inf\", math.inf)\n_register_custom_builtin(\"nan\", \"from math import nan\", math.nan)\n_register_custom_builtin(\"NoneType\", \"NoneType = type(None)\", type(None))\n_register_custom_builtin(\"torch\", \"import torch\", torch)\n_register_custom_builtin(\"device\", \"from torch import device\", torch.device)\n_register_custom_builtin(\"fx_pytree\", \"import torch.fx._pytree as fx_pytree\", fx_pytree)\n_register_custom_builtin(\"pytree\", \"import torch.utils._pytree as pytree\", pytree)\n\n\ndef _is_magic(x: str) -> bool:\n    return x.startswith(\"__\") and x.endswith(\"__\")\n\n\ndef _snake_case(s: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Transforms the given string ``s`` to a Python-style variable name\n\n    Examples:\n        ``mod.snake_case`` -> ``mod.snake_case``\n        ``mod.pascalCase``-> ``mod.pascal_case``\n        ``mod.ALL_CAPS`` -> ``mod.all_caps``\n    \"\"\"\n    return _snake_case_sub(s).lower()\n\n\n# Replace occurrences where a lowercase letter is followed by an uppercase letter\n_snake_case_sub = functools.partial(re.compile(r\"(?<=[a-z])([A-Z])\").sub, r\"_\\1\")\n\n# Find chars that can't be in a Python identifier\n_illegal_char_regex = re.compile(\"[^0-9a-zA-Z_]+\")\n\n# Combined check for variable names:\n# 1) Checks name is not empty\n# 2) Checks first character is not a digit\n# 3) Checks name has no illegal characters (_illegal_char_regex)\n# 3) Splits off the number suffix (if present)\n_name_regex = re.compile(r\"^([a-zA-Z_][0-9a-zA-Z_]*?)(?:_(\\d+))?$\")\n\n# starts with torch but does not start with torch._dynamo. or torch._inductor.\n_torch_but_not_dynamo = re.compile(\n    r\"^torch(?:\\.(?!_dynamo\\.|_inductor\\.)[^.]+)*$\"\n).fullmatch\n\n\ndef _is_from_torch(obj: Any) -> bool:\n    module_name = getattr(obj, \"__module__\", None)\n    if module_name is not None:\n        return _torch_but_not_dynamo(module_name) is not None\n\n    name = getattr(obj, \"__name__\", None)\n    # exclude torch because torch.torch.torch.torch works. idk mang\n    if name is not None and name != \"torch\":\n        for guess in [torch, torch.nn.functional]:\n            if getattr(guess, name, None) is obj:\n                return True\n\n    return False\n\n\nclass _Namespace:\n    \"\"\"A context for associating names uniquely with objects.\n\n    The following invariants are enforced:\n    - Each object gets a single name.\n    - Each name is unique within a given namespace.\n    - Names generated do not shadow builtins, unless the object is indeed that builtin.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._obj_to_name: dict[Any, str] = {}\n        self._used_names: set[str] = set()\n        self._base_count: dict[str, int] = {}\n\n    def create_name(self, candidate: str, obj: Optional[Any]) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Create a unique name.\n\n        Arguments:\n            candidate: used as the basis for the unique name, relevant to the user.\n            obj: If not None, an object that will be associated with the unique name.\n        \"\"\"\n        if obj is not None and obj in self._obj_to_name:\n            return self._obj_to_name[obj]\n\n        # optimistically check if candidate is already a valid name\n        match = _name_regex.match(candidate)\n        if match is None:\n            # delete all characters that are illegal in a Python identifier\n            candidate = _illegal_char_regex.sub(\"_\", candidate)\n\n            if not candidate:\n                candidate = \"_unnamed\"\n\n            if candidate[0].isdigit():\n                candidate = f\"_{candidate}\"\n\n            match = _name_regex.match(candidate)\n            assert match is not None\n\n        base, num = match.group(1, 2)\n        if num is None or candidate in self._used_names:\n            num = self._base_count.get(candidate, 0)\n            if _illegal_names.get(candidate, obj) is not obj:\n                num += 1\n                candidate = f\"{base}_{num}\"\n                # assume illegal names don't end in _\\d so no need to check again\n        else:\n            num = int(num)\n\n        while candidate in self._used_names:\n            num += 1\n            candidate = f\"{base}_{num}\"\n\n        self._used_names.add(candidate)\n        self._base_count[base] = num\n        if obj is not None:\n            self._obj_to_name[obj] = candidate\n        return candidate\n\n    def associate_name_with_obj(self, name: str, obj: Any):\n        \"\"\"Associate a unique name with an object.\n\n        Neither `name` nor `obj` should be associated already.\n        \"\"\"\n        maybe_existing = self._obj_to_name.setdefault(obj, name)\n        assert maybe_existing is name, \"obj is already associated\"\n\n    def _rename_object(self, obj: Any, name: str):\n        assert obj in self._obj_to_name\n        self._obj_to_name[obj] = name\n        self._used_names.add(name)\n\n\n@compatibility(is_backward_compatible=True)\n@dataclass\nclass PythonCode:\n    \"\"\"\n    Represents all the information necessary to exec or save a graph as Python code.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    # Python source code for the forward function definition.\n    src: str\n    # Values in global scope during execution of `src_def`.\n    globals: dict[str, Any]\n    # Optional mapping from the forward function's line number to\n    # node index.\n    _lineno_map: Optional[dict[int, Optional[int]]]\n\n\ndef _format_target(base: str, target: str) -> str:\n    elems = target.split(\".\")\n    r = base\n    for e in elems:\n        if not e.isidentifier():\n            r = f'getattr({r}, \"{e}\")'\n        else:\n            r = f\"{r}.{e}\"\n    return r\n\n\nclass _InsertPoint:\n    def __init__(self, graph, new_insert):\n        self.graph = graph\n        self.orig_insert, graph._insert = graph._insert, new_insert\n\n    def __enter__(self):\n        pass\n\n    def __exit__(self, type, value, tb):\n        self.graph._insert = self.orig_insert\n\n\nclass _node_list:\n    def __init__(self, graph: \"Graph\", direction: Literal[\"_prev\", \"_next\"] = \"_next\"):\n        assert direction in (\"_next\", \"_prev\")\n        self.graph = graph\n        self.direction = direction\n\n    def __len__(self):\n        return self.graph._len\n\n    def __iter__(self):\n        return _NodeIter(self.graph._root, self.direction == \"_prev\")\n\n    def __reversed__(self):\n        return _node_list(self.graph, \"_next\" if self.direction == \"_prev\" else \"_prev\")\n\n\nclass _PyTreeInfo(NamedTuple):\n    \"\"\"\n    Contains extra info stored when we're using Pytrees\n    \"\"\"\n\n    orig_args: list[str]\n    in_spec: pytree.TreeSpec\n    out_spec: Optional[pytree.TreeSpec]\n\n\n@dataclass(frozen=True)\nclass _ParsedStackTrace:\n    \"\"\"\n    Represents the top-most frame of a parsed stack trace\n    \"\"\"\n\n    file: str\n    lineno: str\n    name: str\n    code: str\n\n    def get_summary_str(self):\n        return f\"File: {self.file}:{self.lineno} in {self.name}, code: {self.code}\"\n\n\n# get File:lineno code from stack_trace\ndef _parse_stack_trace(stack_trace: str):\n    if stack_trace is None:\n        return None\n    pattern = re.compile(r\"^File \\\"(.+)\\\", line (\\d+), in (.+)$\")\n    lines = stack_trace.strip().split(\"\\n\")\n    # stacktrace should have innermost frame last, so we\n    # iterate backwards to find the first line that starts\n    # with 'File '\n    for idx in range(len(lines) - 2, -1, -1):\n        line = lines[idx].strip()\n        matches = pattern.match(line)\n        if matches:\n            file = matches.group(1)\n            lineno = matches.group(2)\n            name = matches.group(3)\n            # next line should be the code\n            code = lines[idx + 1].strip()\n            return _ParsedStackTrace(file, lineno, name, code)\n    return None\n\n\n@compatibility(is_backward_compatible=False)\nclass CodeGen:\n    # This is an override hook so we can customize the SymNode printer.\n    _sym_repr: Callable[[\"torch.types.PySymType\"], str] = lambda x: repr(x)\n\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._body_transformer: Optional[TransformCodeFunc] = None\n        self._func_name: str = \"forward\"\n\n    def _format_multiline_args(self, args: list[str]) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Helper to format function arguments in expanded multiline format.\"\"\"\n        return \"\".join(self._format_single_arg(arg) for arg in args)\n\n    def _format_single_arg(self, arg: str) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Helper to format a single argument with optional comment.\"\"\"\n        if \"#\" in arg:\n            arg_part, comment_part = arg.split(\"#\", 1)\n            return f\"    {arg_part.rstrip()},  # {comment_part.lstrip()}\\n\"\n        else:\n            return f\"    {arg},\\n\"\n\n    def _get_delimiters(self, container) -> tuple[str, str]:\n        \"\"\"Helper to get opening and closing delimiters for containers.\"\"\"\n        return (\"(\", \")\") if isinstance(container, tuple) else (\"[\", \"]\")\n\n    def _format_multiline_container(self, items, descs=None, prefix=\"\") -> str:\n        \"\"\"Helper to format containers (lists/tuples) in multiline format.\"\"\"\n        ldelim, rdelim = self._get_delimiters(items)\n        desc_trailers = self._get_desc_trailers(items, descs)\n\n        return (\n            f\"{prefix}{ldelim}\\n\"\n            + \"\".join(\n                f\"    {item},{trailer}\\n\" for item, trailer in zip(items, desc_trailers)\n            )\n            + f\"{rdelim}\"\n        )\n\n    def _get_desc_trailers(self, items, descs):\n        \"\"\"Helper to generate description trailers for items.\"\"\"\n        if descs is None:\n            return [\"\"] * len(items)\n        return [f\"  # {desc}\" for desc in descs]\n\n    def _call_method_with_signature_check(self, method, *args, **kwargs):\n        \"\"\"Helper to call a method with optional parameters based on signature.\"\"\"\n        sig = inspect.signature(method)\n        # Filter kwargs to only include parameters that exist in the method signature\n        filtered_kwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items() if k in sig.parameters}\n        return method(*args, **filtered_kwargs)\n\n    def gen_fn_def(\n        self,\n        free_vars: list[str],\n        maybe_return_annotation: str,\n        *,\n        expanded_def: bool = False,\n    ) -> str:\n        \"\"\"\n        Given the free variables and a return annotation, generates the beginning of the FX function.\n        By default, `gen_fn_def(['a', 'b'], '') == 'def {self._func_name}(a, b):'`\n        \"\"\"\n        # If the original function didn't have self as its first argument, we\n        # would have added it.\n        if len(free_vars) == 0 or free_vars[0] != \"self\":\n            free_vars.insert(0, \"self\")\n\n        if expanded_def:\n            args_formatted = self._format_multiline_args(free_vars)\n            return (\n                f\"def {self._func_name}(\\n{args_formatted}){maybe_return_annotation}:\"\n            )\n        else:\n            return f\"def {self._func_name}({', '.join(free_vars)}){maybe_return_annotation}:\"\n\n    def generate_output(\n        self, output_args: Argument, *, descs: Optional[Any] = None\n    ) -> str:\n        \"\"\"\n        Given the output arguments, generates the return statement of the FX function.\n        Note: The returned statement should not be indented.\n        \"\"\"\n        if descs is not None and isinstance(output_args, (list, tuple)):\n            return self._format_multiline_container(output_args, descs, \"return \")\n        else:\n            return f\"return {repr(output_args)}\"\n\n    def process_inputs(self, *args: Any) -> Any:\n        \"\"\"\n        Transforms the inputs so that the graph can take them as arguments, as\n        non-default codegen may result in the inputs to the function being\n        different from the inputs to the graph.\n\n        If the graph was directly runnable, this invariant should hold true\n        `f.graph.process_outputs(f.graph(*f.graph.process_inputs(*inputs))) == f(*inputs)`\n        \"\"\"\n        return args\n\n    def process_outputs(self, outputs: Any) -> Any:\n        \"\"\"\n        Transforms the outputs of the graph to be identical to the codegen.\n\n        See ``process_inputs`` for more details.\n        \"\"\"\n        return outputs\n\n    def additional_globals(self) -> list[tuple[str, Any]]:\n        \"\"\"\n        If your codegen uses extra global values, add tuples of (identifier,reference to the value) here.\n        For example, return ['List', typing.List] if you need ``List`` in the global context.\n        \"\"\"\n        return []\n\n    def _gen_python_code(\n        self,\n        nodes,\n        root_module: str,\n        namespace: _Namespace,\n        *,\n        verbose: bool = False,\n        include_stride: bool = False,\n        include_device: bool = False,\n        colored: bool = False,\n        # Render each argument on its own line\n        expanded_def: bool = False,\n    ) -> PythonCode:\n        free_vars: list[str] = []\n        body: list[str] = []\n        globals_: dict[str, Any] = {}\n        wrapped_fns: dict[str, None] = {}\n\n        # Wrap string in list to pass by reference\n        maybe_return_annotation: list[str] = [\"\"]\n        include_stride = include_stride or (\n            os.environ.get(\"FX_GRAPH_SHOW_STRIDE\", \"0\") == \"1\"\n        )\n        include_device = include_device or (\n            os.environ.get(\"FX_GRAPH_SHOW_DEVICE\", \"0\") == \"1\"\n        )\n\n        def add_global(name_hint: str, obj: Any):\n            \"\"\"Add an obj to be tracked as a global.\n\n            We call this for names that reference objects external to the\n            Graph, like functions or types.\n\n            Returns: the global name that should be used to reference 'obj' in generated source.\n            \"\"\"\n            if (\n                _is_from_torch(obj) and obj != torch.device\n            ):  # to support registering torch.device\n                # HACK: workaround for how torch custom ops are registered. We\n                # can't import them like normal modules so they must retain their\n                # fully qualified name.\n                return _get_qualified_name(obj)\n\n            # normalize the name hint to get a proper identifier\n            global_name = namespace.create_name(name_hint, obj)\n\n            if global_name in globals_:\n                assert globals_[global_name] == obj\n                return global_name\n            globals_[global_name] = obj\n            return global_name\n\n        # Pre-fill the globals table with registered builtins.\n        for name, (_, obj) in _custom_builtins.items():\n            add_global(name, obj)\n\n        def type_repr(o: Any):\n            if o == ():\n                # Empty tuple is used for empty tuple type annotation Tuple[()]\n                return \"()\"\n\n            typename = _type_repr(o)\n\n            if origin_type := getattr(o, \"__origin__\", None):\n                # list[...], typing.List[...], TensorType[...]\n\n                if isinstance(o, typing._GenericAlias):  # type: ignore[attr-defined]\n                    # This is a generic pre-PEP585 type, e.g. typing.List[torch.Tensor]\n                    origin_type = _origin_type_map.get(origin_type, origin_type)\n\n                origin_typename = add_global(_type_repr(origin_type), origin_type)\n\n                if hasattr(o, \"__args__\"):\n                    # Assign global names for each of the inner type variables.\n                    args = [type_repr(arg) for arg in o.__args__]\n\n                    if len(args) == 0:\n                        # Bare type, such as `typing.Tuple` with no subscript\n                        # This code-path used in Python < 3.9\n                        return origin_typename\n\n                    return f\"{origin_typename}[{','.join(args)}]\"\n                else:\n                    # Bare type, such as `typing.Tuple` with no subscript\n                    # This code-path used in Python 3.9+\n                    return origin_typename\n\n            # Common case: this is a regular module name like 'foo.bar.baz'\n            return add_global(typename, o)\n\n        if colored:\n            red = _color_fns[\"red\"]\n            dim_green = _color_fns[\"dim_green\"]\n            dim = _color_fns[\"dim\"]\n            dim_blue = _color_fns[\"dim_blue\"]\n            blue = _color_fns[\"blue\"]\n        else:\n            red = _identity\n            dim_green = _identity\n            dim = _identity\n            dim_blue = _identity\n            blue = _identity\n\n        def _get_repr(arg: Any) -> str:\n            if isinstance(arg, Node):  # first because common\n                return repr(arg)\n            elif isinstance(arg, tuple) and hasattr(arg, \"_fields\"):\n                # Handle NamedTuples (if it has `_fields`) via add_global.\n                qualified_name = _get_qualified_name(type(arg))\n                global_name = add_global(qualified_name, type(arg))\n                return f\"{global_name}{repr(tuple(arg))}\"\n            elif isinstance(\n                arg, (torch._ops.OpOverload, torch._ops.HigherOrderOperator)\n            ):\n                qualified_name = _get_qualified_name(arg)\n                global_name = add_global(qualified_name, arg)\n                return f\"{global_name}\"\n            elif isinstance(arg, enum.Enum):\n                cls = arg.__class__\n                clsname = add_global(cls.__name__, cls)\n                return f\"{clsname}.{arg.name}\"\n            elif isinstance(arg, torch.Tensor):\n                size = list(arg.size())\n                dtype = str(arg.dtype).split(\".\")[-1]\n                return f\"torch.Tensor(size={size}, dtype={dtype})\"\n            elif isinstance(arg, tuple):\n                if len(arg) == 1:\n                    return f\"({_get_repr(arg[0])},)\"\n                else:\n                    return \"(\" + \", \".join(_get_repr(a) for a in arg) + \")\"\n            elif isinstance(arg, list):\n                return \"[\" + \", \".join(_get_repr(a) for a in arg) + \"]\"\n            elif isinstance(arg, slice):\n                return f\"slice({_get_repr(arg.start)}, {_get_repr(arg.stop)}, {_get_repr(arg.step)})\"\n            else:\n                return blue(repr(arg))\n\n        def _format_args(\n            args: tuple[Argument, ...], kwargs: dict[str, Argument]\n        ) -> str:\n            res = [_get_repr(a) for a in args]\n            res.extend([f\"{k} = {_get_repr(v)}\" for k, v in kwargs.items()])\n            return \", \".join(res)\n\n        # Run through reverse nodes and record the first instance of a use\n        # of a given node. This represents the *last* use of the node in the\n        # execution order of the program, which we will use to free unused\n        # values\n        node_to_last_use: dict[Node, Node] = {}\n        user_to_last_uses: dict[Node, list[Node]] = {}\n\n        def register_last_uses(n: Node, user: Node):\n            if n not in node_to_last_use:\n                node_to_last_use[n] = user\n                user_to_last_uses.setdefault(user, []).append(n)\n\n        for node in reversed(nodes):\n            for input_node in node._input_nodes:\n                register_last_uses(input_node, node)\n\n        def delete_unused_values(user: Node):\n            \"\"\"\n            Delete values after their last use. This ensures that values that are\n            not used in the remainder of the code are freed and the memory usage\n            of the code is optimal.\n            \"\"\"\n            if user.op == \"placeholder\":\n                return\n            if user.op == \"output\":\n                body.append(\"\\n\")\n                return\n            nodes_to_delete = user_to_last_uses.get(user, [])\n\n            if len(user.users.keys()) == 0:\n                # This node is not used by any others. however it's also not\n                # removed by DCE since side-effect. We want to free it's outputs\n                # right after its execution done to save memory.\n                nodes_to_delete.append(user)\n\n            if len(nodes_to_delete):\n                to_delete_str = \" = \".join(\n                    [repr(n) for n in nodes_to_delete] + [\"None\"]\n                )\n                body.append(f\";  {dim(to_delete_str)}\\n\")\n            else:\n                body.append(\"\\n\")\n\n        prev_stacktrace = None\n\n        def append_stacktrace_summary(node: Node):\n            \"\"\"\n            Append a summary of the stacktrace to the generated code. This is\n            useful for debugging.\n            \"\"\"\n            nonlocal prev_stacktrace\n\n            if node.op not in {\"placeholder\", \"output\"}:\n                stack_trace = node.stack_trace\n                if stack_trace:\n                    if stack_trace != prev_stacktrace:\n                        prev_stacktrace = stack_trace\n                        if parsed_stack_trace := _parse_stack_trace(stack_trace):\n                            summary_str = parsed_stack_trace.get_summary_str()\n                        else:\n                            summary_str = \"\"\n                        body.append(f\"\\n {dim(f'# {summary_str}')}\\n\")\n                elif prev_stacktrace != \"\":\n                    prev_stacktrace = \"\"\n                    no_stacktrace_msg = \"# No stacktrace found for following nodes\"\n                    body.append(f\"\\n{dim(no_stacktrace_msg)}\\n\")\n\n        def stringify_shape(shape: Iterable) -> str:\n            return f\"[{', '.join([str(x) for x in shape])}]\"\n\n        def emit_node(node: Node):\n            maybe_type_annotation = (\n                \"\" if node.type is None else f\" : {type_repr(node.type)}\"\n            )\n            maybe_comment = \"\"\n\n            if verbose:\n                # override annotation with more detailed information\n                from
